coredata.dc:title: A dataset for large prismatic lithium-ion battery cells (CALB L148N58A): Comprehensive characterization and real-world driving cycles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Fasolato, Simone
Blasuttigh, Nicola
Galuppini, Giacomo
Raimondo, Davide M.
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents an experimental dataset for a batch of eleven prismatic CALB L148N58A lithium-ion B-grade battery cells with a nominal capacity of 58 Ah. The experimental campaign, conducted at the Energy Laboratory for Interdisciplinary Storage Applications (ELISA) at the University of Trieste, Italy, employs non-destructive tests to assess the performance of each cell within the batch. The cell-level testing procedures include fixed Constant Current Constant Voltage (CCCV) charging and Constant Current (CC) discharging at low current rates, Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) tests at various C-rates (i.e., 1C and C/3), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) at different State of Charge (SOC) levels, and three distinct driving cycles (WLTP, UDDS and US06). All the experiments were conducted at three different ambient temperatures (10°C, 25°C, and 40°C), resulting in a comprehensive dataset for assessing the performance metrics of the battery cells. This dataset provides valuable insights into post-manufacturing cell-to-cell variations in performance metrics such as capacity and impedance within a batch of fresh cells. Additionally, it serves as a crucial resource for developing battery models, including physics-based, empirical, and data-driven approaches. Moreover, it may contribute to validate model-based and data-driven estimation and control strategies within battery management systems, enhancing the reliability and efficiency of energy storage solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Cell characterization
Hybrid pulse power characterization
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Driving cycle

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research presented within this paper was partially supported by European Union through the EcoMobility project (grant 101112306). The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the Energy Laboratory for Interdisciplinary Storage Applications (ELISA) for hosting the experimental activities. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

The research presented within this paper was partially supported by European Union through the EcoMobility project (grant 101112306). The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the Energy Laboratory for Interdisciplinary Storage Applications (ELISA) for hosting the experimental activities.

) used to calculate the ohmic resistance (

head.ce:title: A dataset for large prismatic lithium-ion battery cells (CALB L148N58A): Comprehensive characterization and real-world driving cycles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Computer and Biomedical Engineering, University of Pavia, Pavia, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Electrical, Computer and Biomedical Engineering, University of Pavia, Pavia, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Engineering and Architecture, University of Trieste, Trieste, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Engineering and Architecture, University of Trieste, Trieste, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Energy, Environment and Transport Giacomo Ciamician, University of Trieste, Trieste, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCenter for Energy, Environment and Transport Giacomo Ciamician, University of Trieste, Trieste, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents an experimental dataset for a batch of eleven prismatic CALB L148N58A lithium-ion B-grade battery cells with a nominal capacity of 58 Ah. The experimental campaign, conducted at the Energy Laboratory for Interdisciplinary Storage Applications (ELISA) at the University of Trieste, Italy, employs non-destructive tests to assess the performance of each cell within the batch. The cell-level testing procedures include fixed Constant Current Constant Voltage (CCCV) charging and Constant Current (CC) discharging at low current rates, Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) tests at various C-rates (i.e., 1C and C/3), Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) at different State of Charge (SOC) levels, and three distinct driving cycles (WLTP, UDDS and US06). All the experiments were conducted at three different ambient temperatures (10°C, 25°C, and 40°C), resulting in a comprehensive dataset for assessing the performance metrics of the battery cells. This dataset provides valuable insights into post-manufacturing cell-to-cell variations in performance metrics such as capacity and impedance within a batch of fresh cells. Additionally, it serves as a crucial resource for developing battery models, including physics-based, empirical, and data-driven approaches. Moreover, it may contribute to validate model-based and data-driven estimation and control strategies within battery management systems, enhancing the reliability and efficiency of energy storage solutions.

ce:list-item.ce:para: CALB L148N58A testing campaign A Dataset for Large Prismatic Lithium-Ion Battery Cells (CALB L148N58A): Comprehensive Characterization and Real-World Driving Cycles (Original data) .

ce:sections.ce:para:
Specifications Table
Subject Electrical and Electronic Engineering.
Specific subject area Cell characterization and real-driving tests for a batch of fresh prismatic lithium-ion batteries.
Type of data Excel files (.xlsx) - Raw data
MATLAB files (.mat) - Processed data
Data collection The batch of eleven prismatic cells is tested using the Arbin LBT21084HC system as cyclers. The Arbin system is also utilized for measuring cell terminal voltages and surface temperatures, with the latter sensed using T-type thermocouples. The ESPEC LU-114 thermal chamber maintains the experimental ambient temperature at the designated target. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests are conducted using the Gamry 3000AE. The testing protocols are implemented, and data is logged using MITS Pro Software. Each test is exported in .xlsx files (Raw Data), while the processed data, extracted directly from the raw data without any filtering or resampling, are derived using Matlab.
Data source location
Institution: Department of Engineering and Architecture, University of Trieste.
City: Trieste.
Country: Italy.
Latitude and longitude for collected samples/data:
(45.659695368356246, 13.79468340502675)
Data accessibility
Repository name: CALB L148N58A testing campaign
Data identification number: DOI: 10.17632/ycx459r5c3.1
Direct URL to data: https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/ycx459r5c3/1
Related research article None

ce:list-item.ce:para: The proposed dataset can offer valuable insights in determining whether the 58 Ah CALB L148N58A [1] can meet the energy and/or power requirements of specific applications, particularly in the sectors of electric vehicles and renewable energy integration.

The single-cell characterization, including charge/discharge at low current rate, HPPC tests, and EIS, is performed on a total of eleven single cells at three different ambient temperatures, 10°C, 25°C and 40°C using a thermal chamber. The comprehensive experimental campaign aims to identify the distribution of post-manufacturing cell-to-cell variations in terms of capacity and impedance within a batch of fresh prismatic cells.

The dataset provides cell-level data that enables the development of battery models, including physics-based, empirical, and data-driven approaches by including voltage, current, and cells temperature data under different environmental conditions. A comprehensive dataset encompassing diverse experimental conditions is essential for mitigating identifiability challenges associated with various model topologies [2–3].

The standard single-cell characterization data are enhanced by testing the eleven cells under real-world driving cycles. Specifically, the Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP), Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS), and US06 cycles are appropriately scaled to match the operating current range of the tested cells. This experimental profile aims to provide suitable validation data for model-based and data-driven estimation and control strategies within the context of battery management systems. Particularly, the collected cell measurements (i.e. current and voltage) can be used as reference inputs for evaluating the accuracy and robustness of estimation [5] and control [6] strategies under realistic conditions, eliminating the need to rely solely on synthetic data.

The integration of both standard and dynamic characterization allows for a comprehensive evaluation of cell performance across different factors, enhancing the ability to simulate real-life scenarios for electric vehicles and stationary energy storage systems.

ce:section.ce:para: As the demand for reliable and efficient energy solutions continues to grow, especially with the rise of electric vehicles, renewable energy integration, and portable electronics, understanding the performance and limitations of batteries is more crucial than ever. In this context, battery experimental campaigns are essential for assessing performance metrics such as capacity, energy density, and power output, with the ultimate aim of determining whether a battery can meet the requirements of specific applications. This dataset presents the characterization tests of a batch of eleven CALB L148N58A prismatic cells [1]. Specifically, the considered cells feature a graphite-based negative electrode, and a Nickel-Manganese-Cobalt (NMC)-based positive electrode. The experimental campaign for these cells includes galvanostatic capacity tests conducted at low C-rates, Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) tests, Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), and real-world driving cycle testing protocols. Further, each test is conducted at three different ambient temperatures, 10°C, 25°C and 40°C, in order to investigate how these affect the cells performance. The dataset provides valuable insights into the cells performance under various conditions and supports the further development of battery models, including physics-based, empirical, and data-driven approaches. Particularly, a comprehensive dataset encompassing diverse experimental conditions is essential for mitigating identifiability challenges associated with various model topologies [2–3]. Further, it can be utilized to validate estimation and control strategies within battery management systems [4–6].

This dataset includes battery experimental tests for a batch of eleven prismatic B-graded CALB L148N58A [1] with nominal capacity of 58 Ah, whose technical specifications are included in Table 1 for completeness. Each cell in the batch is assigned a unique ID number, with the complete list provided in the last row of Table 1. Notably, in the experiments, cell terminal voltages of 4.2 V and 2.5 V were selected to represent 100% and 0% states of charge, respectively.

The experimental process for the 11 cells is divided into four main sections, namely C/20, HPPC, driving cycles and EIS, as outlined in Table 2 . It is important to note that these sections are repeated at three different ambient temperatures: 10, 25, and 40°C. The selected values are well-established benchmark temperatures that replicate realistic operating conditions, allowing the direct comparisons of our cell properties with those reported in other studies [7–8]. The sections are:

1. Constant current C/20 cycle: this procedure involves both discharging and charging the battery at a C/20 rate using a constant current (CC). The complete testing steps are listed in Table 2, Steps 1–10. Before starting the C/20 cycle, each cell undergoes a conditioning phase where it is charged using a constant current–constant voltage (CCCV) protocol at a rate of C/4, followed by a CC discharge at the same rate of C/4. In this conditioning phase, the charging is performed at constant current until the voltage reaches 4.2 V; then, during the CV phase, voltage is maintained at 4.2 V until the charging current decreases to 100 mA. After 1 hour of rest, the C/3 discharge phase begins, and it ends when the cell voltage drops to 2.5 V. After a 30-minute rest period, the cell is charged again using CCCV at C/20, in accordance with Step 6-7 in Table 2 and visually represented in Fig. 1 ①. Finally, after a 60-minutes rest, the fully charged cell is discharged down to a cutoff voltage of 2.5 V during the C/20 CC discharge phase shown in Fig. 1②.

To assess the consistency of the collected data, the capacity extracted during the CC C/20 discharge (Step 9 in Table 2) is calculated as [9]:
Q
d i s
=
1 3600
∫
t
i n
t
f i n
I
c e l l
d t
where
t
i n
and
t
f i n
are the initial and final time instants of the discharge, and
I
c e l l
is the cell current.

Fig. 2
(a) shows the voltage curves during the C/20 constant current discharge phase for the entire batch and for different temperatures. As can be seen, the discharge time varies across the cell batch, ranging from 18 to 19 hours. Even at the same ambient temperature, the discharge times are different, leading to different capacity values. This behaviour is highlighted by the boxplot in Fig. 2 (b), which visually illustrates the percentiles of
Q
d i s
, calculated for the batch of 11 cells at the three tested temperature, is visually illustrated. As expected, the median capacity value increases as the temperature rises, while the range is larger at low temperatures [4].

1. Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC) test at various C-rates: the protocol of the HPPC test was designed to assess the impact of the SOC on the internal properties of the cells [9–11]. This test involves applying a pair of discharge and charge current pulses at different SOCs. The two pulses are followed by a longer battery discharge phase to obtain evenly spaced SOC values, as illustrated in Fig. 1③-④. The complete testing steps are listed in Table 2, Steps 11–23. Initially, the battery is fully charged using a C/4 CCCV cycle (Steps 11–12). Then, a sequence of 10-second discharge and charge constant current (CC) pulses is applied, with a 10-minute rest period after each pulse (Step 13). Note that the amplitude of the charging pulse is selected in accordance with [10] to be 70% of the 1C-rate current value. Subsequently, the battery is discharged at a 1C rate for 6 minutes (Step 14) to establish the next desired SOC, followed by a resting period of 60 minutes to allow the battery voltage to relax (Step 15). Finally, Steps 13–15 are repeated 10 times in a loop. Note that this procedure is repeated using two different C-rates for the discharging pulses: 1C (Steps 11–16) and C/3 (Steps 17–23), as visually illustrated in Fig. 1 ③ and ④, respectively.

To assess the consistency of the collected data, the HPPC test are used to extract the cell ohmic resistance for the batch of 11 cells at each tested temperature [9–11]. According to Fig. 3
(b), the discharge ohmic resistance (
R
0 , d i s
) is determined by the ratio of the instantaneous voltage drop (
Δ
V
p u l s e
) to the instantaneous current drop (
Δ
I
p u l s e
) that occurs at the moment of the current pulse [9]:
R
0 , d i s
=
Δ
V
p u l s e
Δ
I
p u l s e

The
R
0 , d i s
trend is obtained as a function of the SOC at the different ambient temperatures is depicted in Fig. 3 (c). Note that the ohmic resistance of cell “60403” is slightly above the expected trend, as highlighted by the outliers (the circular points). As expected, the data highlights that the cell resistance tends to be higher at lower temperatures. Moreover, as SOC increases, the data show a slight decrease in resistance for all temperatures, then a slight increase again toward reaching maximum capacity.

1. Driving cycles: Three different driving cycles (DVs) are analysed: the Worldwide Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP), the Urban Dynamometer Driving Schedule (UDDS), and the US06 cycle. Each cycle represents a standardized speed profile over time and is typically used to compare fuel consumption and performance in conventional combustion vehicles. In the context of EVs, time-varying current profiles are generated from an electric vehicle simulator to simulate the battery pack input current in real driving conditions. These profiles are then scaled for a single cell based on battery pack topology, providing a realistic representation of actual current patterns. The WLTP and UDDS are global driving cycle standards, each lasting 30 minutes, while the US06 duration is 12 minutes. In this campaign, to extend the experimental duration and cover a broader SOC range, multiple WLTP, UDDS, and US06 cycles are concatenated where each ends when the battery voltage drops to 2.5 V, as shown in Fig. 4 and listed in Table 2, Steps 24-35.

Additionally, the current profiles are scaled to achieve a maximum peak current of 1C-rate, and different initial conditions are considered. Before each DV cycle, a C/4 CCCV cycle is applied to set the desired SOC value. Then, the sequence of the considered dynamic current profile is applied. Specifically, the initial SOC is set at approximately 100% for the WLTP, 80% for the UDDS, and 60% for the US06 cycle, as noted in Steps 24, 28 and 32 in Table 2, respectively.

2. EIS test: The EIS test assesses battery impedance [9,11] across a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 3 kHz at four different SOC levels: 20%, 40%, 60%, and 80%. The testing steps, detailed in Table 2 (Steps 36–42) and illustrated in Fig. 5 for 10°C temperature, proceed as follows. Initially, the battery is fully charged using a C/4 CCCV cycle (Steps 36-37). After a 30-minutes resting period (Step 38), the battery is discharged at a 1C rate for 12 minutes (Step 39) to achieve the desired SOC. Then, the cell rests for 90 minutes to allow voltage relaxation before the EIS measurement is performed (Steps 40-41). Steps 39 through 41 are repeated four times to measure the cell impedance at each SOC level.

The root folder containing all the files is named “CALB L148N58A testing campaign” and is divided into the two sub-folders:
1.
Raw Data
2.
Processed Data
An exhaustive description of each sub-folder is offered below, and schematically depicted in Fig. 6 .

This folder contains characterization data collected for each cell in the batch at three different temperatures: 10°C, 25°C, and 40°C. The main folder is organized into three subfolders corresponding to these testing temperatures, named “Temperature_10C”, “Temperature_25C”, and “Temperature_40C”. Each of these temperature-specific subfolders contains 11 additional subfolders, one for each tested cell, labeled with the respective cell ID as illustrated in Fig. 6. Within each cell ID folder, there are three further subfolders named “C20_HPPC_cycles”, “Driving_cycles”, and “EIS_tests”. In the “C20_HPPC_cycles” folder, the C/20 charging and discharging cycles are stored, along with the HPPC cycle conducted at both C/3 and 1C rates, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The “Driving_cycles” folder contains the sequence of driving cycles, including WLTP, UDDS, and US06, as shown in Fig. 4. Finally, the “EIS_tests” folder includes the EIS tests at different SOC levels as shown in Fig. 5.

It is worth noting that these latter subfolders hold the raw data, which are saved as Excel spreadsheets with the .xlsx extension, directly obtained from the MITS Pro software. The column headers in the excel spreadsheets correspond to the sensed signals during the experiments. Among the most relevant signals, we can distinguish:
•
Date_Time: Logged testing time in “day/month/year hour:minutes:seconds” format
•
Test_Times: Logged time profile in seconds from the test protocol start
•
Step_Times: Logged time profile in seconds resetting at each Step_Index increment
•
Step_Index: Flag indicating the step number reached in the test protocol
•
Cycle_Index: Flag indicating the cycle number reached in the test protocol
•
Voltage (V): Terminals voltage profile in Volt
•
Current (A): Supplied cell current profile in Ampere
•
Aux_Temperature_1 (C): Cell surface temperature profile in°C
•
Aux_Temperature_2 (C): Thermal chamber ambient temperature profile in°C
Please note that each final subfolder may contain multiple Excel files, typically two or three. This is because the MITS Pro software splits the output file when its size becomes too large.

This folder contains the processed characterization data collected for each cell in the batch at three different temperatures: 10°C, 25°C, and 40°C. It is important to note that the processed data are extracted directly from the raw data without any filtering or resampling. To facilitate quick data analysis, the raw data have been converted to .mat files to be used with Matlab.

The main folder is organized into three subfolders corresponding to these testing temperatures, named “Temperature_10C”, “Temperature_25C”, and “Temperature_40C”. Each of these temperature-specific subfolders contains eight additional subfolders, each representing a specific testing condition extracted from the raw data. Specifically, the processed data are divided into:
•
C20_Charge: contains the CCCV charging cycle at C/20 rate, Steps 6-7 in Table 2
•
C20_Discharge: contains the CC discharging cycle at C/20 rate, Step 9 in Table 2
•
HPPC_1C: contains the HPPC with C/3 discharging pulses, Step 13 in Table 2
•
HPPC_C3: contains the HPPC with 1C discharging pulses, Step 19 in Table 2
•
DV_WLTP: contains the WLTP driving cycle, Step 26 in Table 2
•
DV_UDDS: contains the UDDS driving cycle, Step 30 in Table 2
•
DV_US06: contains the US06 driving cycle, Step 34 in Table 2
•
EIS_test: contains the ESI test at different SOC levels, Step 38 in Table 2
Within each subfolder, the processed data are stored in .mat files labelled with the cell ID number, formatted as “CELL_ID.mat” (i.e. 59294.mat).

The equipment available at the Energy Laboratory for Interdisciplinary Storage Applications (ELISA) employed in the experimental campaign is shown in Fig. 7 . Specifically, the cell testing schedules are configured using MITS Pro software, which defines the experimental protocols and sequences. To supply the battery cell with the desired current profile and to collect sensor data (e.g. cell voltage, and cell surface temperatures), the Arbin LBT21084HC is used in conjunction with the Data Acquisition System (DAQ). During testing, each cell is placed inside the ESPEC LU-114 thermal chamber and is equipped with T-type thermocouples to measure the ambient temperature as well as the surface temperature of each cell, as shown in Fig. 7. The Gamry 3000AE is directly connected to the host computer (via USB link) and integrated with the Arbin cycler to perform EIS tests at different SOC levels: this allows EIS measurements to be performed from the MITS Pro software (Version 8.00.PV 221201) by directly exploiting the Arbin cables without any disconnection. Finally, each test is exported in .xlsx files. It is important to further note that the data visualizations presented in Figs. 1-5 were derived using MatLab R2021b.
The authors confirm that this work meets the ethical requirements of the journal.

Simone Fasolato: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Nicola Blasuttigh: Supervision, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Giacomo Galuppini: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Davide M. Raimondo: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research presented within this paper was partially supported by European Union through the EcoMobility project (grant 101112306). The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of the Energy Laboratory for Interdisciplinary Storage Applications (ELISA) for hosting the experimental activities.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: CALB battery specifications rechargeable lithium ion cell: CALB L148N58A,”. [Online]. Available: https://calb-battery.us/calb-l148n58a-58ah.html.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel interval-based approach for quantifying practical parameter identifiability of a lithium-ion battery model

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, W., Huang, R., Liu, K., & Zhang, W. (2020). A novel interval-based approach for quantifying practical parameter identifiability of a lithium-ion battery model. Int. J. Energy Res., 44(5), 3558-3573.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nonlinear identifiability analysis of multiphase porous electrode theory-based battery models: a lithium iron phosphate case study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Galuppini, G., Berliner, M. D., Cogswell, D. A., Zhuang, D., Bazant, M. Z., & Braatz, R. D. (2023). Nonlinear identifiability analysis of multiphase porous electrode theory-based battery models: a lithium iron phosphate case study. J. Power. Sources., 573, 233009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery Management Systems, Volume I: Battery Modeling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Plett, Gregory L. Battery Management Systems, Volume I: Battery Modeling. Artech House, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Online capacity estimation for lithium-ion battery cells via an electro chemical model-based adaptive interconnected observer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Allam, A., and Onori, S. (2020). Online capacity estimation for lithium-ion battery cells via an electro chemical model-based adaptive interconnected observer. IEEE Trans. Control Syst. Technol., 29(4), 1636-1651.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A deep reinforcement learning framework for fast charging of Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Park, S., Pozzi, A., Whitmeyer, M., Perez, H., Kandel, A., Kim, G., Choi, Y., Joe, W. T. and Raimondo, D. M., and Moura, S. (2022). A deep reinforcement learning framework for fast charging of Li-ion batteries. IEEE Trans. Transport. Electrific., 8(2), 2770-2784.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full factorial design of experiments dataset for parallel-connected lithium-ion cells imbalanced performance investigation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Piombo, G., Fasolato, S., Heymer, R., Hidalgo, M. F., Niri, M. F., Raimondo, D. M., Marco, J. and Onori, S. (2024). Full factorial design of experiments dataset for parallel-connected lithium-ion cells imbalanced performance investigation. Data Brief., 53, 110227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A multi-stage lithium-ion battery aging dataset using various experimental design methodologies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Stroebl, F., Petersohn, R., Schricker, B., Schaeufl, F., Bohlen, O., Palm, H. (2024). A multi-stage lithium-ion battery aging dataset using various experimental design methodologies. Sci. Data, 11(1), 1020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical characterization tools for lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ha, S., Pozzato, G., & Onori, S. (2024). Electrochemical characterization tools for lithium-ion batteries. J. Solid State Electrochem., 28(3), 1131-1157.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery Test Manual for Electric Vehicles, Revision 3 (No. INL/EXT-15-34184)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Christophersen, J. P. (2015). Battery Test Manual for Electric Vehicles, Revision 3 (No. INL/EXT-15-34184). Idaho National Lab.(INL), Idaho Falls, ID (United States).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparison of methodologies for the non-invasive characterisation of commercial Li-ion cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Barai, A., Uddin, K., Dubarry, M., Somerville, L., McGordon, A., Jennings, P., & Bloom, I. (2019). A comparison of methodologies for the non-invasive characterisation of commercial Li-ion cells. Prog. Energy Combust. Sci., 72, 1-31.
